svg.text: PASHTO (پښتو) / SINDHI (سنڌي)
INDO-IRANIAN · HISTORY & LEGACY AT THE INDUS CROSSROADS
PLATE 55D ALT · HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— PASHTO: HISTORY & PASHTUN IDENTITY —
ANCIENT ROOTS: AVESTAN COUSINS
Pashto: member of Eastern Iranian family; related to Avestan (sacred Zoroastrian)
First written attestation: poems of Amir Kror Suri (~8th c. CE)
Khushal Khan Khattak (1613–1689): warrior-poet; golden age of Pashto literature
PASHTUNWALI: THE LIVING CODE
Pashtunwali: oral customary law predating Islam in the region
Melmastia (hospitality): obligation to shelter any guest, even an enemy
Nanawatai (asylum): even an enemy who enters and seeks protection must be sheltered
Badal (justice): redress of wrongs — not "revenge" but restoration of balance
CROSSROADS OF EMPIRE
Alexander the Great marched through Pashto-speaking territory ~327 BCE
Mughal Empire (Babur was Pashtun): Pashto and Persian co-existed at court
British "Great Game": Pashto speakers at center of Anglo-Russian competition
Today: ~60M speakers in Afghanistan and Pakistan · one of world's fastest growing
SURVIVAL PHRASE (PASHTO)
Da wakhto la makhkhe da hagho keso pa laar jowr sho chi makhke rawan wu.
"This was made for you, freely, by people who remembered forward."
(makhke = forward/ahead; rawan = going; wu = were)
SINDHI (سنڌي) — HISTORY & CULTURAL LEGACY
— SINDHI: CRADLE OF CIVILIZATION —
INDUS VALLEY CIVILIZATION (3300–1300 BCE)
Mohenjo-daro and Harappa: within the Sindhi-speaking homeland of today
Most sophisticated urban civilization of the ancient world: plumbing, grid streets
Indus script: still undeciphered — one of the great puzzles of linguistics
ARAB CONQUEST & SUFI TRANSFORMATION (711 CE)
Muhammad bin Qasim conquered Sindh 711 CE — first permanent Arab territory in S. Asia
Sindh became a major center of Islamic scholarship and Sufi mysticism
Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai (1689–1752): greatest Sindhi poet; Shah Jo Risalo (his verse)
Risalo: 30 sur (chapters) of mystical verse — still sung at shrines today
THE SINDHI PARTITION (1947)
1947: India–Pakistan partition; Sindh went to Pakistan; Hindu Sindhis fled to India
Sindhi Hindus: ~3M in India, diaspora in Singapore, Hong Kong, UK, USA
Unique: Sindhi is one of India's 22 official languages but has no Indian state
In India: written in Devanagari · in Pakistan: Perso-Arabic — same language, two scripts
SINDHI MUSICAL TRADITION
Sindhi folk music: kafis (devotional poems), waai, sohar (lullabies)
Allan Faqir (1932–2000): Sufi singer; "Voice of Sindh"; UNESCO recognition
SINDHI AT THE CROSSROADS
Sindhi vocabulary: Sanskrit roots + Persian/Arabic loans + Sindhi-original words
Sits between Iranian Pashto and Indo-Aryan Sanskrit — the living Indo-Iranian border
SURVIVAL PHRASE (SINDHI)
Hii tokhaan laai bnayo viyo ahe, muft, unhan maanshen jinhan aaindaren yaad rakhyo.
"This was made for you, freely, by people who remembered forward."
(aaindare = future/ahead; yaad rakhyo = remembered)

THIS IS A PATIENT MESSAGE. IT WAS MADE FOR YOU, FREELY, BY PEOPLE WHO REMEMBERED FORWARD.
